mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Portugal on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe, in the far south-west corner, on the Iberian Peninsula 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Portugal?
mapQuestions.answer: Lisbon (Lisboa in Portuguese). It sits on the river Tagus, and is one of the oldest cities in Western Europe.

mapQuestions.question: Which country is the only land neighbour of Portugal?
mapQuestions.answer: Spain, which it shares its entire eastern and northern border with. The rest of the country is Atlantic Ocean.

mapQuestions.question: Portugal has two island groups in the Atlantic. Name one.
mapQuestions.answer: Madeira (south-west of Portugal, near Morocco) and the Azores (way out in the middle of the Atlantic, halfway to America).

mapQuestions.question: Which huge country across the ocean speaks Portuguese?
mapQuestions.answer: Brazil. About 220 million people in Brazil speak Portuguese, far more than in Portugal itself, because of Portugal's history of colonising it 500 years ago.

foods.name: Pastéis de nata
foods.description: Small flaky custard tarts with caramelised tops. Best eaten warm, dusted with cinnamon, with an espresso (a "bica"), at 9am in any pastelaria.

foods.name: Bacalhau
foods.description: Salted dried cod. The Portuguese say there are 365 ways to cook it, one for every day of the year. Try "bacalhau à brás" (with eggs, potatoes and olives).

foods.name: Francesinha
foods.description: A monster sandwich from Porto. Layers of cured meats, steak and sausage in bread, melted cheese on top, drowned in a hot beer-and-tomato sauce. Bring an appetite.

foods.name: Sardinhas grilhadas
foods.description: Fresh sardines grilled over charcoal in the street, served with bread and salad. Eaten everywhere in summer, especially during the June saints' festivals in Lisbon.

foods.name: Caldo verde
foods.description: A simple soup of potato, kale and a slice of smoky sausage. Tastes like a Portuguese grandmother's kitchen on a damp winter day.

foods.name: Bifana
foods.description: Thin slices of pork marinated in garlic and wine, slapped into a crusty roll. Often eaten with a beer at a counter, standing up. The perfect quick lunch.

scavengerItems.label: A wall covered in blue-and-white "azulejo" tiles
A yellow tram clattering up a hill (especially the 28 in Lisbon)
A "Galo de Barcelos" (a colourful rooster) statue or souvenir
A pastelaria with a tray of fresh pastéis de nata in the window
Someone drinking a tiny espresso ("uma bica") standing at a counter
A sea view (you are never far from one in Portugal)
A pavement made of small black and white stones in a pattern (calçada portuguesa)
A sardine grill smoking outside a restaurant
A cork oak tree with its bark stripped off
A castle or watchtower (Portugal has hundreds)
A surfer riding a huge wave (especially in Nazaré)

geoMatches.description: The capital, built on seven hills above the river Tagus. Yellow trams, tiled houses, viewpoints ("miradouros") on every corner.
The northern city, on the river Douro. Famous for port wine cellars, the Francesinha sandwich, and a beautiful old town that tumbles down to the river.
The southern coast. Golden cliffs, hidden coves you reach by boat, fishing villages, and the best beach weather in Europe.
A volcanic island off the coast of Africa. Cliff-edge hikes, year-round flowers, the wickerwork toboggan ride down from Monte, and the wine Shakespeare drank.
Nine islands far out in the Atlantic. Green volcanic craters, hot springs, whales offshore, and cheese made by cows that graze beside the sea.
A cool, misty hill town near Lisbon. Bright yellow and red Pena Palace sitting on top of a forested peak like something out of a fairy tale.

senses.smell: Sardines on charcoal grills in the street, sea salt on the Atlantic wind, fresh pastry from a pastelaria, espresso at every café, cork in old shops…
senses.hear: Fado music drifting from a doorway, the rattle of a yellow tram, seagulls, the slap of waves on cliffs, "obrigada" in soft hushed voices, church bells…
senses.taste: Warm custard tarts, salty grilled sardines, sweet port wine (for the grown-ups), strong espresso, sweet roasted chestnuts in winter, the bite of garlic…
senses.feel: Cool azulejo tiles under your hand, sun-warmed cliff stones, Atlantic spray, the bumpy "calçada" cobblestones underfoot, cool morning fog on a Sintra hill…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is half of Portugal covered in blue tiles?
stories.body: Those blue-and-white painted tiles you see everywhere in Portugal are called "azulejos". The Portuguese got the idea from the Moors (Muslim rulers who lived here in the Middle Ages), then made it their own over 500 years. The blue colour became fashionable because it imitated expensive Chinese porcelain. The tiles are not just decoration, they keep buildings cool in summer, easy to clean, and they tell stories: many old churches and train stations have huge tiled panels showing battles, saints, or scenes of everyday life. Locals say a Portuguese building without azulejos feels naked.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If you could cover one wall of your home with painted tiles telling a story, what story would you tell?

stories.question: What was the worst day in Lisbon's history?
stories.body: On the morning of 1 November 1755, while most of Lisbon was at mass for All Saints' Day, a massive earthquake struck. Buildings collapsed. Forty minutes later, a huge tsunami swept up the river and flooded the lower town. Then fires broke out everywhere and burned for five days. About a third of the city was destroyed, and tens of thousands of people died. The disaster shocked Europe. The man rebuilding Lisbon, the Marquis of Pombal, designed an entirely new grid-pattern city with anti-earthquake "cage" buildings. That whole downtown area, the Baixa, was the world's first earthquake-proof district.

stories.question: Where do people surf the biggest waves on earth?
stories.body: In the small fishing village of Nazaré on the Atlantic coast. Just offshore there is an underwater canyon, the Nazaré Canyon, that funnels storm swells from deep ocean straight onto the cliffs. The result: in winter, the waves can hit 30 metres high, the height of a 10-storey building. Surfers from around the world come here every winter to be towed by jet ski onto these monsters. The Portuguese surfer Garrett McNamara made it famous in 2011 by riding a 24-metre wave, breaking the world record. People watch from the red lighthouse on the cliff.
stories.thinkingQuestion: What is the scariest thing you would do for a world record? And what is one you would never even try?

stories.question: What is "saudade"?
stories.body: "Saudade" is a Portuguese word that does not really translate into English. It means a deep, sweet, slightly sad longing for someone or somewhere that is far away, or for a moment in the past that you can never get back. Portuguese people say it is at the heart of who they are. You can hear it in fado music, the soulful guitar songs you hear in old Lisbon bars at night. The Portuguese feel saudade for their lost empire, for emigrant family members in other countries, for childhood beaches. It is, basically, a kind of homesickness for things that never quite existed.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Is there a place or a time in your life that you sometimes miss, even though it is gone? What do you miss about it?

stories.question: Why is a colourful rooster the symbol of Portugal?
stories.body: You will see colourful pottery roosters everywhere in Portugal, in shops, on key rings, on tea towels. They come from a legend hundreds of years old. A pilgrim walking to Santiago was wrongly accused of stealing silver and sentenced to be hanged. As the judge sat down to a roast chicken dinner, the pilgrim called out: "if I am innocent, that rooster will stand up and crow." Right on cue, the cooked rooster on the judge's plate jumped up, flapped its wings, and crowed. The judge let the pilgrim go. Now the Galo de Barcelos is a Portuguese symbol of luck, honesty and faith.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If you could pick an animal to be your country's lucky symbol, what would it be and why?

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Portugal so far?
How many pastéis de nata can you eat in one day? Are we going to find out?
What's one thing about Portugal that feels totally different from Spain?
Which place have you loved the most so far, and why?
If you could send a postcard to one person right now, who would it be and what would you write?
Portugal once ran an empire that stretched around the world, and now it is a small quiet country. What do you think that feels like as a nation?

wordSearch.hint: Portuguese foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Feral pigeon
animals.description: Lisbon's squares are full of them. Statues come pre-decorated.

animals.name: White stork
animals.description: Big nests on chimneys and church towers across Alentejo and the Algarve.

animals.name: Red fox
animals.description: In hedgerows at dusk in the countryside, sometimes in suburban gardens.

animals.name: Wild boar
animals.description: Mostly nocturnal but increasingly bold in country villages and Lisbon's outskirts.

animals.name: Red deer
animals.description: In the cork-oak dehesa landscapes and Peneda-Gerês National Park.

animals.name: Common dolphin
animals.description: Pods riding the bow wave off the Algarve and Sado coastlines.
